questions.subject: NSEP
questions.q: An ac source (sinusoidal source with frequency 50 Hz ) is connected in series with a rectifying diode, a $100 \Omega$ resistor, a $1000 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ capacitor and a milliammeter. After some time the milliammeter reads zero. The voltage measured across the capacitor with a dc voltmeter is
options: the peak voltage of the ac source.
rms voltage of the ac source.
average voltage of the ac source over a half cycle.
average voltage of the ac source over a full cycle.

questions.subject: NSEP
questions.q: A simple laboratory power supply consists of a transformer, bridge rectifier and a filter capacitor. It drives a suitable load. If due to some reason one of the diodes in the rectifier circuit becomes open, then
options: output voltage of power supply falls to zero.
output voltage of power supply decreases to some nonzero value.
ac ripple in the output increases.

questions.q: For the Boolean equation $Y=A B+A(B+C)+B(B+C)+\bar{B}$, which of the following statements is correct?

questions.subject: NSEP
questions.q:
In a p-n junction diode, the current (i) varies with applied biasing voltage (V) and can be expressed as $i=i_0\left(e^{\mathrm{q} \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{kT}}-1\right)$ where $\mathrm{i}_0=5 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~A}$ is reverse saturation current, k is Boltzmann constant and $q$ is the charge on the electron
At Absolute Temperature $\mathrm{T}=300 \mathrm{~K}$
options: The forward current is approximately 59.5 mA for a forward bias of 0.6 volt
The current increases approximately by 2.75 A if the biasing voltage changes from 0.6 V to 0.7 V
The dynamic resistance of p-n junction is approximately $435 \mathrm{~m} \Omega$ at the biasing voltage of 0.6 V
The change in reverse bias current when biasing voltage change from -1 volt to -2 volt happens to be practically zero.

questions.subject: NSEP
questions.q:
Statement I: The current flowing through a p-n junction is more in forward bias than that in the reverse bias.
Statement II: The diffusion current, dominant in forward bias, is more than the drift current, dominant in the reverse bias.
options: If statement I is true and statement II is true and also if the statement II is a correct explanation of statement I
If statement I is true and statement II is true but the statement II is a not a correct explanation of statement I
If statement I is true but the statement II is false
If statement I is false but statement II is true

questions.q: A potential of 5 V is applied across the faces of a pure germanium plate of area $2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$ and of thickness $1.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$. Concentration of carriers in germanium at room temperature is $1.6 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$, Mobility of electrons and holes are $0.4 \mathrm{~m}^2 \mathrm{~V}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ and $0.2 \mathrm{~m}^2 \mathrm{~V}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ respectively. The current produced in germanium plate at room temperature, is

questions.q: In the working of a $p-n$ junction
options: diffusion current dominates when the junction is forward biased
drift current dominates when the junction is reverse biased
depletion region width decreases with increase in forward bias voltage.
the electric field in the depletion region depends on the number of ionized dopants rather than the dopant density.
